data.title: Coromandel International to Acquire 53% in NACL Industries for INR8.2 Billion
data.description:
Coromandel International has agreed to acquire a 53% stake in agrochemical manufacturer NACL Industries for 8.20 billion Indian rupees, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The shares will be acquired at 76.7 rupees price per share from NACL's current promoter KLR Products.

The company will also launch an open offer to buy an additional up to 26% stake in NACL by the second quarter of fiscal year 2025-26.

The acquisition will help to expand Coromandel's scale and expedite its entry into contract manufacturing business, and new product commercialization along with expanding its product portfolio.

NACL operates technical and formulation plants in Andhra Pradesh, India and has a centralized research and development facility near Hyderabad.

Shares of Coromandel International were up over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Saudi Ground Services Co.'s FY24 Net Profit, Revenue Up
data.description:
Saudi Ground Services Co. (SASE:4031) said Thursday its net profit and revenue for the full year 2024 rose year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 327 million Saudi riyals, compared with 211.5 million riyals a year ago. EPS moved to 1.74 riyals from 1.12 riyals.

The Saudi Arabia-listed passenger and aircraft ground-handling services company's revenue was 2.68 billion riyals, compared with 2.46 billion riyals a year ago.

data.title: First Milling Co. Posts Higher FY24 Net Profit, Revenue
data.description:
First Milling Co. (SASE:2283) said Thursday that its net profit and revenue for full-year 2024 increased year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 250.9 million Saudi riyals, compared with 220.2 million riyals a year ago. EPS moved to 4.53 riyals from 3.97 riyals.

The Saudi Arabia-listed milling company's revenue was 1.05 billion riyals, compared with 964.3 million riyals earlier.

data.title: Gold Cup Electric Apparatus to Build Production Base in Czech Republic for 700 Million Yuan
data.description:
Gold Cup Electric Apparatus plans to invest 700 million yuan to construct an electromagnetic wire production base in the Czech Republic, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange disclosure on Wednesday.

The factory, which will be built in two phases, will produce 20,000 tons of electromagnetic wire every year after its construction is completed in 2028.

The construction will implemented by Gold Cup Tongli European Electromagnetic Wire, a subsidiary that will be established through the Chinese wire and cable manufacturer's Singaporean unit, Gold Cup Electric Apparatus International Investment, the disclosure said.

data.title: Thailand approves $1 billion investment from China's Sunwoda for EV battery plant
data.description:
Thailand's Investment Board said on Thursday it had approved a $1 billion investment from China's Sunwoda Electronic to build an electric vehicle battery plant, the latest in a series of new EV-related projects in the regional automaking hub.

The Shenzhen-based firm will create 1,000 jobs in Thailand for production of lithium-ion battery cells for EV manufacturers at a facility that would include manufacturing, research and product development.

Thailand is Southeast Asia's largest auto production and export centre, home to major carmakers like Toyota and Honda. But the sector is the midst of a major slump, however, with Thai autos output down by a tenth last year to a four-year low and domestic sales and exports slumping 26% and 8.8% respectively.

In recent years, Chinese EV makers like BYD and Great Wall Motor and others have poured more than 102.7 billion baht ($3 billion) into the country, according to the EV industry association.

The government this week said it plans tax incentives for plug-in hybrid vehicles and credit guarantees for pick-up truck purchases. Japanese automaker are also seeking government support for a car trade-in and scrapping scheme to stimulate sales, Reuters reported last month.

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: Volkswagen's Software Unit Cariad Plans 1,600 Layoffs
data.description:
(Updates to add Volkswagen's statement)

Volkswagen's software unit Cariad is planning to lay off 1,600 employees, or 30% of its workforce, German newspaper Handelsblatt reported on Tuesday, citing company sources.

The layoffs will take place by the end of 2025 after workers were informed at a staff meeting on Tuesday.

German automaker Volkswagen told MT Newswires it made Cariad more efficient in 2024 "as an internal software solutions developer with a higher ratio of in-house development and are now also adjusting the number of employees accordingly."

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Anhui Tongyuan Environment Energy Saving Nabs Wastewater Treatment, Waste Remediation Bids; Shares Fall 5%
data.description:
Anhui Tongyuan Environment Energy Saving secured two major projects for wastewater treatment and waste remediation.

The 924.9 million-yuan EPCO contract for Gansu Juhua New Materials involves wastewater treatment and industrial water facilities, according to a Thursday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Meanwhile, the 107.5 million-yuan EPC contract in Daiyue District, focuses on landfill waste remediation.

Shares of the environment services provider were down over 5% in recent trade.

data.title: Berenberg Ups Air Liquide's Price Target on Backlog Strength, Cost Management
data.description:
Berenberg on Wednesday held Air Liquide at buy, raising its price target to 200 euros from 190 euros, citing strong backlogs and "achievable" cost savings.

The analyst also raised its projections for the French industrial and healthcare gases company's EPS over higher margins due to better cost management and net pricing, especially in Europe. For 2025 through 2027, EPS estimates are raised to 6.87 euros, 7.58 euros, and 8.03 euros, respectively, from 6.85 euros, 7.37 euros, and 7.84 euros.

"The company should be able to further improve margins over the next two years thanks to enhanced cost management. The backlog, a proxy for future earnings growth, is also at historically high levels," the analyst said. "Air Liquide's net debt/EBITDA is also the lowest of the large industrial gas makers, which may in two to three years open the door to the novelty of buying back stock in addition to the more familiar increases in dividend."

data.title: Home Product Center's Accelerated Store Openings Likely to Secure Sales Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Home Product Center's accelerated store openings are likely to secure sales growth, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Suttatip Peerasub says in a research report as the brokerage maintains the stock's buy rating. The home improvement retailer wants to open 12 stores in 2025 versus nine stores in 2024, with expansion mostly in hybrid format with 'HomePro' and "Mega Home' businesses at same locations, the analyst notes. Hybrid stores reduce investment costs by 20%-25%, leading to savings on operating expenditure per store. However, the brokerage trims its 2025-2026 earnings forecasts for the Thailand company by 2% to reflect increasing selling, general, and administrative expenses. It lowers its target price to THB10.20 from THB13.00.Shares are 1.9% higher at THB7.90. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: ASX Poised for Potential Upside, JPMorgan Says
data.description:
ASX is set for potential upside even in a volatile market, driven by stable earnings, effective cost control, and the ability to hike fees to offset substantial capital expenditure, according to a Thursday report by the Australian, citing JP Morgan.

JPMorgan analyst Siddharth Prameswaran said ASX is currently trading at a premium to the ASX 200 and noted that its operating cost control appears to be outperforming the full-year outlook.

The brokerage upgraded ASX's rating to overweight.

data.title: Shenzhen Composite Index Extends Decline; Last 1.7% Lower at 2054.26
VA Tech Wabag Bags Orders Worth INR3.60 Billion
data.description:
VA Tech Wabag has secured orders worth 3.60 billion Indian rupees from Gail India and Indian Oil Corporation , according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The order from Gail worth 3.40 billion rupees involves the design, construction and operation of a 450 cum/hr UF & RO-based effluent recycle plant, evaporator-based zero liquid discharge plant, and a new waste water treatment plant and augmentation of an existing wastewater treatment plant at its Pata petrochemical complex in Uttar Pradesh, India.

The company also bagged a repeat order from Indian Oil worth 200 million Indian rupees for the operation and maintenance of a tertiary treatment reverse osmosis plant at its Panipat refinery in Haryana, India, for an additional period of three years.

Shares of the company were up nearly 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Tariff worries weigh as CPI cheer fades
data.description:
A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Kevin Buckland

Wall Street's mild bounce on the back of a tame CPI reading did not provide much impetus for Asian markets, leaving European investors with little cause for optimism.

One problem is that the inflation data, while offering some relief from the run of discouraging indicators lately, won't translate directly into a lower PCE price index - the Fed's preferred gauge of price pressures - because the cooling came mostly in services.

Another concern is that February's data doesn't fully capture the impact of President Donald Trump's wave of tariffs.

And ultimately, the primary worry for markets is not inflation, but growth.

On the subject of tariffs, Europe finds itself directly in Trump's sights after the EU's threat of counter-measures was met by a warning of reciprocal duties from the United States.

It remains to be seen whether Trump's approach to Europe mirrors the ramp-up-and-reprieve strategy used for Canada and Mexico, or the tax-and-then-tax-again model applied to China.

The good news is that, according to Germany's Kiel Institute, only a "small fraction" of targeted products from the EU are exported to the United States.

Meanwhile, Britain continues to keep a relatively low profile, refraining from immediate tariff retaliation but keeping all options open.

That may be why sterling continues its steady climb while the euro is slipping back, albeit after a steeper ascent.

A majority of Americans believe Trump's economic policy has been too erratic, and an even greater number expect higher prices as a result.

Warnings from corporate America are also surfacing, with airline Delta and retail giant Walmart indicating that the unusually high level of economic uncertainty will impact their profits.

A potential de-escalation in Trump's trade war could be sown at home rather than due to tit-for-tat tariffs from major trading partners. However, a more than 10% slide in the S&P 500 in just three weeks doesn't seem to be prompting a reconsideration of U.S. policy.

Key developments that could influence markets on Thursday:

- UK RICS housing survey (Feb)

- Sweden CPI (Feb)

- Euro area industrial production (Jan)

- US PPI (Feb), weekly jobless claims

data.title: Merck and 8 More Healthcare Stocks for Nervous Dividend Investors — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Al Root

Slower inflation, lower bond yields, and economic upheaval make playing defense a defensible investment strategy for the year ahead — and there may be no better place to start building a healthy dividend portfolio than the healthcare sector.

In 2024, playing defense meant owning the Magnificent Seven, which gained an average 60%. Not this year — the group is down an average 24% from their respective 52-week highs. Dividend stocks, however, are holding up just fine — the SPDR S&P Dividend exchange-traded fund has returned 2.5% so far this year — and healthcare stocks are doing even better: The Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) has returned 6.2%.

That outperformance makes complete sense. Dividend-paying stocks are a traditional defensive play, while healthcare faces fewer trade and economic headwinds than most sectors. Combined, they've done even better. S&P 500 healthcare stocks with the highest yields have returned almost 10% year to date, while those that pay relatively meager dividends have lost investors about 3% on average. The stocks without quarterly payouts have lost about 4% year to date. In other words, they're two great tastes that go great together.

There's also a fundamental case to be made for healthcare stocks, lest you worry they'll underperform once the market gets its mojo back. Medical-device makers are seeing improved growth "underpinned by demographic tailwinds, with baby boomers aging in the silver tsunami," writes Jefferies analyst Matthew Taylor. Hospital pricing and utilization trends look steady, too, according to Mizuho analyst Ann Hynes. Even pharmaceutical companies are expected to do well, with those in the S&P 500 posting sales growth of almost 10% in the fourth quarter, dramatically better than a year ago.

Where to start? CVS Health, Merck, AbbVie, and Gilead Sciences look particularly attractive. Almost 70% of analysts covering the stocks rate them Buy, above the 55% average for all companies in the S&P 500. Wall Street expects the quartet to grow earnings in 2025 by 20% on average. Their balance sheet leverage looks reasonable, and their dividend payouts have amounted to a safe 60% of income earned over the past 12 months.

The four stocks also look cheap. They trade for about 13 times estimated 2025 earnings, below the S&P 500's 21 times multiple, and yield about 3.3% on average, compared with 2.3% for the average healthcare stocks in the Russell 1000 index that pay dividends.

Investors comfortable with lower yields could consider insurer UnitedHealth Group, medical-device companies Abbott Laboratories and Stryker, technology provider Becton Dickinson, and distributor Cardinal Health. They yield an average of 1.6% and have paid out less than 50% of income as dividends over the past 12 months. Their balance sheets also look strong and they are expected to grow earnings by 9% on average, driven by industry tailwinds. Wall Street likes them too. More than 80% of analysts covering the shares rate them Buy. They trade for about 20 times estimated 2025 earnings on average, a little less expensive than the broader market.

Investors preferring to avoid the work associated with evaluating and managing individual stocks can always look to ETFs to adjust sector exposure. There are some differences to note. The Health Care Select Sector SPDR yields about 1.7%, better than the 1.3% yield for iShares U.S. Healthcare (IYH) and the 1.4% yield for Vanguard Health Care (VHT).

No matter how investors decide to play them, dividend-paying healthcare stocks could be the perfect prescription for market volatility.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Elife's Chairman, Vice-Chairmen Removed From Office Amid Board Overhaul
data.description:
Elife Holdings' recently elected chairman Qiu Bin was removed from his position from the board after just one week, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The company also disclosed the removal of Co-Vice Chairmen Lee Tsung Ta and Chen Xiaodong and numerous former directors, as well as the placement of co-CEOs on administrative leave. The vice chairmen were elected on March 5.

The board shakeup follows an extraordinary general meeting held on Tuesday.
